coredata.dc:title: Facile green preparation of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from bagasse for sensitive methyl parathion detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Ji
Huang, Guoxia
Li, Jie
Li, Junsheng
Yan, Liujuan
Wei, Jianfeng
Zhang, Qian
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a high sensitivity fluorescence sensor system utilizing nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) from bagasse was studied. A method was developed to examine methyl parathion (MP) via alkali hydrolysis. N-CDs were prepared via hydrothermal method from biomass bagasse. The maximum excitation band of the N-CDs was 370 nm, and the fluorescence quantum yield was 32.6 %. In a strong alkaline environment, the MP is hydrolyzed into p-nitrophenol via electrostatic interactions between inner filtration effect and molecules, which can inhibit the fluorescence of the N-CDs. The established MP-N-CD system has good linearity, with 0.005–10.0 μg·mL−1 bound and a detection limit of 0.83 ng·mL−1. Then a fluorescence sensing system was used to detect MP in vegetable and fruit samples. The recoveries were between 98.2 % and 101.9 %, and relative standard deviation decreased to 3.6 %. The proposed method is green, simple, cost-effective, highly sensitive, and highly selective.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM images of the N-CDs; (b) histogram statistical distribution of the sizes; (c, d) TEM images of the N-CDs.
Survey (a), high-resolution C1s (b), N1s (c), and O1s (d) XPS spectra of the as-prepared N-CDs.
a–d: Fluorescence and UV spectra of N-CDs synthesized from bagasse: a, b: undoped CDs; c, d: N-CDs diluted 5000 times; e: XRD pattern; f: FTIR spectra of the CDs.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with increasing MP content (1–11: 0.0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.08, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 7.5, and 10.0 μg·mL−1 MP); (b) Corresponding linearity curves of the F0/F and MP contents.
(a) Fluorescence attenuation curve of N-CDs with and without p-NP; (b) ultraviolet spectra of p-NPs and fluorescence of N-CDs.
Process chart for the synthesis of bagasse-based N-CDs and the detection of MPs.
Comparative results of fluorescent sensors based on the inner filtration effect with reported MP measurement methods.

row.entry: Tap water, lettuce, cabbage, pear and rice

head.ce:title: Facile green preparation of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from bagasse for sensitive methyl parathion detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Green Processing of Sugar Resources, College of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University of Science and Technology, Liuzhou 545006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Green Processing of Sugar Resources, College of Biological and Chemical Engineering
Guangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Green Processing of Sugar Resources, College of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University of Science and Technology, Liuzhou 545006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a high sensitivity fluorescence sensor system utilizing nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) from bagasse was studied. A method was developed to examine methyl parathion (MP) via alkali hydrolysis. N-CDs were prepared via hydrothermal method from biomass bagasse. The maximum excitation band of the N-CDs was 370 nm, and the fluorescence quantum yield was 32.6 %. In a strong alkaline environment, the MP is hydrolyzed into p-nitrophenol via electrostatic interactions between inner filtration effect and molecules, which can inhibit the fluorescence of the N-CDs. The established MP-N-CD system has good linearity, with 0.005–10.0 μg·mL−1 bound and a detection limit of 0.83 ng·mL−1. Then a fluorescence sensing system was used to detect MP in vegetable and fruit samples. The recoveries were between 98.2 % and 101.9 %, and relative standard deviation decreased to 3.6 %. The proposed method is green, simple, cost-effective, highly sensitive, and highly selective.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Methyl parathion (MP) is extensively utilized in agricultural products for crop pest control (Majer & Finsgar, 2022; Kaur et al., 2023; Ajiboye, Oladoye et al., 2022). Nevertheless, the cumulative use of MPs has increasingly raised concerns about food security because of the high toxicity and bioaccumulation of MPs in human health and the environment. The World Health Organization classifies it as toxic substance, and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) designates MP as a class I toxic pesticide. MP inhibits acetylcholinesterase in human body, causing excessive accumulation of acetylcholine in tissues and resulting in headache, nausea, diarrhea, and abdominal colic (Lan et al., 2019). Moreover, MP can lead to organ failure and death due to disorders of the nervous system (He et al., 2023; Xue et al., 2024). For food security to be monitored, a simple, sensitive, and reliable approach for MP analysis is needed to establish and improve pesticide detection systems.

The main methods used to detect MPs include high-performance liquid chromatography (Jalalzaei et al., 2024), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Almutairi, Alsaleem, Herbish, Sayari, & Alowaifeer, 2021), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (Mehmandoust et al., 2023), gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (Nakamura et al., 2023). The advantages of these methods are their sensitivity, good selectivity, and accurate and reliable results. However, chromatographic detection instruments are expensive, complex, time-consuming, and require professional personnel to operate and maintain. A variety of electrochemical and optical methods for detecting MPs have been reported, many of which involve enzyme inhibition reactions (Guo et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024; Liang et al., 2022). Acetylcholinesterase is the main enzyme used to construct MP detection biosensors (Xu et al., 2023). However, carbamate insecticides can inhibit cholinesterase and have low selectivity for organophosphorus pesticides (Xu et al., 2024). Organophosphate hydrolase is another common enzyme for MP detection (Wu et al., 2023); its inhibited enzyme activity is affected mainly by different detection conditions and solvent types (Kukkar et al., 2021). In addition, relatively expensive enzymes hinder the wide application of the aforementioned methods. On this basis, an enzyme-free fluorescent sensor for the facile, efficient, and cost-effective determination of MPs must be developed.

As zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have been rapidly developed and used in chemical analysis because of their distinctive optical qualities, good biocompatibility, and low preparation cost. In recent years, fluorescence sensors based on the internal filter effect (IFE) have been proven to be a reliable method for trace detection due to their simple fabrication method and high sensitivity (Lan et al., 2019). Typically, the fluorescence can be quenched by competitive absorption by matching the absorption of the analyte (absorbent) and the excitation/emission of the fluorescent group (Yang et al., 2023). Unlike other fluorescence response mechanisms, the IFE does not involve any electronic or energy transfer processes, so IFE makes the analysis more simplified. Therefore, methyl parathion can be hydrolyzed to p-nitrophenol (p-NP) in alkaline conditions without adding any other reagents or enzymes. p-NP has a strong absorbance at 400 nm, which can quench the fluorescence of CDs by IFE. This is a promising and cost-effective enzyme-free detection method for MP. This technology has been rapidly exploited for the analysis of MP (Lan et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2022; Song et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023). However, the quantum yield (QY) of these nanomaterials is relatively low, thereby limiting their wide application. Bagasse is mainly a byproduct of sugar production, but it also serves as a considerable biomass resource. The annual global sugarcane production is 1.87 billion tons (Parveen et al., 2024). In 2024, the potential global production of bagasse likely reached 600 million tons (Martinez-Hernandez et al., 2018). Following Brazil and India, China's annual sugarcane planting area is the third largest sugarcane grower and sugar producer on earth (Huang et al., 2020). However, approximately 70 % of bagasse is combustible, resulting in many health- and pollution-related problems that are unaligned with the concepts of energy conservation and emission reduction. Therefore, methods for biomass utilization must be improved. As one of the largest crop wastes on earth, bagasse is renewable. In addition, bagasse has the advantages of low price, large yield, centralized source, and simple collection, which is convenient for industrial production. Bagasse is also rich in hydroxyl groups and has great advantages in the preparation of CDs. The CDs prepared from bagasse have broad development prospects.

A new fluorescent sensor that uses nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), which are facile, cost-effective, and efficient for the determination of MPs, is considered in this work. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , N-CDs with high quantum dot yields can be synthesized via hydrothermal methods using bagasse as the raw material and ethylenediamine as the passivator without any modification. MP can be hydrolyzed under alkaline conditions without the addition of enzymes or reagents. This work aims to provide a promising and economical technology for the enzyme-free determination of MPs. The proposed method can be considered a new economical and sustainable strategy for the efficient recycling of solid waste.

Bagasse was acquired from a sugar factory (Liuzhou, Guangxi). The MPs were acquired from Manhag Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ethylenediamine was obtained from Xilong Science Co., Ltd. (China). The reagent purity used in this experiment was analytical purity. Actual samples were obtained from local markets in Liuzhou, Guangxi Province.

UV–Vis spectra were obtained via a Cary-60 UV spectrometer (Agilent Technology, USA). The FTIR spectrum was obtained via a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany). Fluorescence studies were performed on an RF-5301PC fluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted with an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 apparatus (Philips, Holland) utilizing an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was conducted on a Bruker D2-phaser diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with a VGESCALAB 220i-XL system.

The collected bagasse was washed completely and then pulverized with a crusher. A total of 1.0 g of crushed and dried bagasse and 0.20 mL of ethylenediamine were solvated in 30 mL of distilled water for 10 min via ultrasonication. The mixture was subsequently placed into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 7 h. The product was ultrasonically dispersed for 10 min and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove the precipitate. The CDs were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and further purified via a 1000 MWCO dialysis membrane for 24 h to obtain brownish yellow N-CDs. The prepared N-CD solution was placed in a freeze-dry oven for 24 h to obtain a yellow solid (Scheme 1).

The fluorescence QY of the CDs was detected using quinine sulfate (QS) as a reference. F CD and F QS refer to the fluorescence intensities of the CDs and QS, respectively. A CD and A QS denote the absorbance values of CDs and QS, respectively. n represents the refractive index of the solvent (Sun et al., 2022):
(1)
Y CD
=
Y QD
F CD
A QS
n 2
CD
F QS
A CD
n 2
QS
,
where Y CD and Y QS represent the QY of CDs and QS, respectively. F CD and F QS refer to the fluorescence intensity of CDs and QS, individually. A CD and A QS denote the absorbance value of CDs and QS, respectively. n stands for refractive index of solvent (n CD = n QS = 1.33).

Under optimal conditions, 0.2 mL of 5000-fold diluted N-CD solution was added to a 5 mL tube, different MP concentrations were added, and the final volume was 2.5 mL with PBS buffer. The λex was set as 370 nm. The selectivity study was performed with various ions (Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Al3+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ce3+, Hg2+, and Fe2+) (4 μg·mL−1) and ethanol, acetone, and benzene (0.025 mL). The ions and solvents were placed into the test tube containing the N-CDs in the same manner.

Apple, cucumber, and romaine lettuce vegetables were purchased from a local supermarket. Apple, cucumber peels, and vegetable leaves were crushed and extracted via ultrasonication for 1 h. Then, the samples were centrifuged (10,000 rpm), filtered through a membrane (0.22 μm) and attenuated five times with PBS buffer (pH = 12). The contents of 1.00, 3.00 and 5.00 μg∙mL−1 MP were poured into the test tube containing the sample, and the determination was executed under optimized conditions.

The synthesized carbon dot solid was observed via SEM. As shown in Fig. 1a, the N-CDs synthesized via this method are nearly spherical in shape. The histogram in Fig. 1b, which was acquired from the TEM image, illustrates that the diameter of the N-CDs was 2–6 nm, with an average size of 4.2 nm. The granulometric distribution is narrow, which is consistent with the observed TEM data (Fig. 1c). The N-CDs are monodispersed particles with a spherical shape, and their dispersion is good, without aggregation. HRTEM images indicate that most of the particles are amorphous carbon with no distinct lattice streaks (Fig. 1d), similar to other reports (Zare-Dorabei et al., 2016).

XPS spectra were recorded to probe the elemental composition and chemical bonding of the prepared N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2a, the N-CDs contained mainly C, N and O. The XPS data revealed bands at 284.78, 405.95, and 534.76 eV, assigned to C1s, N1s, and O1s electrons, respectively (Fig. 2a). The energy spectrum of C1s displays four bands at 284.4, 285.6, 286.4, and 287.3 eV, representing the CC, CN, CO, and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 2b). The N1s band reveals two bands at 399.7 and 401.6 eV, which are attributed to CN and NH bonds, respectively (Fig. 2c). In the O1s spectrum, the bands at 531.5 and 532.6 eV are attributed to CO and C–OH/C–O–C bonds, respectively (Fig. 2d). XPS characterization and analysis revealed that the content of C in the N-CDs was 80.92 %, the N content was 5.28 %, and the O content was 15.8 %. Therefore, many carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups exist on the surface of the N-CDs. These hydrophilic functional groups induce good dispersion and stability in aqueous solutions (Yang et al., 2023).

The prepared N-CD was tested via XRD to study the degree of crystallinity and phase state. As shown in Fig. 3e, the XRD pattern of the N-CDs presents wide diffraction peaks at approximately 20.5°, revealing the existence of an amorphous carbon structure in the graphite plane. N-CDs have a partially amorphous structure owing to the presence of some nitrogen-containing groups (Huang et al., 2019).

FT-IR spectra were obtained to characterize the functional groups of the N-CDs and undoped CDs. As shown in Fig. 3f, the CD spectrum displays wide peaks at 3435 and 3411 cm−1, indicating the existence of characteristic absorption peaks of residual OH and NH in the bagasse carbon source. The characteristic peak at 2921 cm−1 is the tensile vibration peak of the CH bond. The band values at 1529 and 1637 cm−1 are attributed to the CO–NH bending vibration (Elsherbiny et al., 2022). The peaks at 1458 and 1386 cm−1 can be attributed to the tensile vibration of CN. The bands at 1222 and 1043 cm−1 can be attributed to the bond stretching vibration of CO (Luo et al., 2020). The existence of functionalized nitrogen groups on the N-CDs was verified. In general, the synthesized N-CDs from bagasse are rich in hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl hydrophilic groups.

Studying the absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs is the most convenient method for characterizing CDs. The fluorescence intensity and UV spectra of the prepared CDs were analyzed, with undoped CDs used as controls. As illustrated in Figs. 1a and b, the excitation spectrum of the undoped CDs shows that λex is at 350 nm (Fig. 3b, blue line). When the CDs were excited at 350 nm, λem appeared at 439 nm. When λex increases from 320 to 390 nm, the band intensity initially increases and decreases, then redshifts from 432 to 474 nm (Fig. 3a). This finding can be attributed to the particle size distribution of the CDs. According to the UV absorption spectrum, the UV absorption peak is at a maximum near 270 nm, resulting from the π - π * transition (Atchudan et al., 2019).

Figs. 3c and d show the fluorescence and UV spectra of N-CDs synthesized from bagasse. The excitation band of the N-CDs shows that the λex is 370 nm (Fig. 3c, blue line). The emission band of the N-CDs was stimulated at 370 nm, and emission peaks appeared at 442 nm (Fig. 3d, brown curve). With the addition of λex in the range of 320–400 nm, the maximum emission peak exhibited a redshift of 27 nm, accompanied by a significant reduction in the fluorescence emission signal (Fig. 3c). According to the ultraviolet absorption band of the N-CDs, the maximum UV absorption wavelength was 274 nm. The excitation wavelength-dependent emission of CDs derived from bagasse might be caused by different particle sizes and defects on the CD surface.

The insertions in Figs. 3b and 3d are pictures of the CDs under sunlight and UV lamp irradiation. The CDs exhibited excellent water dispersion. After ultraviolet light irradiation (365 nm), the prepared CDs emit blue light but emit light yellow under sunlight irradiation. Given their bright blue appearance, the prepared CDs can be used as fluorescent ink.

As illustrated in Figs. S1a to e, the fluorescence intensities of the N-CDs slightly varied with varying concentrations of NaCl, pH, UV light irradiation time, temperature, and solvent, revealing their excellent stability and tolerance. The fluorescence intensity was almost the same, and no significant changes were reported even after three months. The intensity of ultraviolet light did not change after 60 min of continuous irradiation. With longer preservation times and continuous ultraviolet irradiation, no photobleaching or coagulation occurred.

According to Eq. (1), the QY of the as-prepared CDs at 350 nm excitation was calculated to be 15.3 %, and the QY of the N-CDs at 370 nm excitation was determined to be 32.6 %. This method is considered useful for economic and green synthesis and utilization of biowaste bagasse as the raw material.

Various possible influencing factors, such as reaction time, temperature and pH, were explored to determine the best detection conditions. The effect of pH on MP determination was investigated. As shown in Fig. S2a, the change in the fluorescence signal of the N-CDs was not obvious at pH 2–10. When the pH was greater than 10, the fluorescence quenching efficiency gradually increased, reaching a maximum at pH 12, showing no significant change as the pH further increased. Therefore, a pH of 12 was selected for the next study. Fig. S2b shows the effects of temperature on MP detection. The quenching efficiency reached a maximum at a reaction temperature of 60 °C and then decreased when the temperature further increased. Therefore, 60 °C was chosen as the optimal detection temperature. Finally, the influence of the reaction time was estimated. The fluorescence quenching efficiency improved gradually and remained constant at 10 min (Fig. S2c), indicating that the procedure was completed in 10 min. Thus, the hydrolysis reaction reached equilibrium at that time. The best reaction time was 10 min.

The sensitivity of N-CD fluorescent probes for the detection of MPs was investigated. The fluorescence spectra of N-CD solutions with different contents of MPs are shown in Fig. 4a. Notably, the fluorescence signal of the N-CDs decreased proportionally as the MP concentration increased. Moreover, the original bright blue color of the system weakened or disappeared under ultraviolet irradiation (visible to the naked eye). Thus, MPs can induce fluorescence quenching in N-CDs. The proposed sensing system can be utilized to detect MPs. As shown in Fig. 4b, a good linear relationship (y = 0.5988× + 1.2337, R 2 = 0.9983) within the range of 0.005–10.0 μg·mL−1 was observed, and the LOD was 0.83 ng·mL−1. On the basis of the guideline limits for MP content (1.0 and 0.05 μg·mL−1) proposed by Japan and the Canada Health Organization, respectively, the LOD for MP in this work was much lower, indicating that the CDs have good potential for the future detection of trace MP. More importantly, the proposed method does not require complex technology, harsh conditions, or expensive equipment.

Several similar pesticides (dimethoate, carbendazim, glyphosate, chlorpyrifos, malathion, methamidophos, and trichlorfon) and common coexisting substances (magnesium sulfate, barium hydroxide, copper acetate, ferric chloride, zinc nitrate, aluminum chloride, potassium chloride, sodium chloride, calcium chloride, chromium nitrate, nickel nitrate, manganese sulfate, silver nitrate, cobalt chloride, lead nitrate, benzoic acid, MP, ascorbic acid, ethanol, acetone, and benzene) were selected as interferences to examine the selectivity of the fluorescent sensing system for MPs. As shown in Fig. S3, only the MP induced a significant change in the fluorescence signal of the N-CDs, and the other interfering substances caused insignificant fluorescence quenching of the N-CDs, which demonstrated that the sensor system has a high degree of selection for detecting MPs.

Compared with the other reported methods listed in Table 1 , the detection limit is smaller than that of the reported methods, indicating that the method used in this study has high sensitivity. The detection procedure is implemented via alkaline catalytic hydrolysis without the addition of reagents or enzymes. Previously reported MP fluorescent probes were synthesized via complex and energy-intensive procedures. However, N-CDs are prepared via a hydrothermal green process. Compared with other detection methods based on CDs in Table 1, N-CDs are prepared via using bagasse, a cost-effective, abundant, and easily collected waste material. N-CDs can be easily prepared from bagasse with high QY. The excellent fluorescence stability and high QY of the N-CDs from bagasse also indicate that the analytical method has good sensitivity, wide detection range and reproducibility. This study provides a simple, rapid, sensitive, and cost-effective method for MP detection. The proposed method has the advantages of good stability, simple operation, low dependence on complex technology and expensive equipment, low cost, and environmental friendliness.

The quenching procedure involves static and dynamic quenching. Static quenching does not affect the fluorescence lifetime of fluorophores, whereas dynamic quenching can lead to a decrease in the lifetime of fluorophores. As shown in Fig. 4a, as the MP concentration increased, the fluorescence signal of the N-CD solution decreased. Thus, the quenching procedure may involve fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or the inner filtration effect (IFE) between the donor and the receptor (Yu et al., 2014). FRET is a nonradiative energy transfer, and the corresponding fluorescence lifetime is shortened or extended. This phenomenon is caused by the IFE, in which the excited spectrum or emission band of the donor overlaps with the absorption spectrum of the receptor to some degree (Zhang, Hassan, et al., 2022).

A fluorescence attenuation curve of the N-CDs was obtained to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the N-CDs by using p-nitrophenol (p-NP). After the addition of p-NP, the fluorescence lifetime of the N-CDs remained stable (Fig. 5a). This method can rule out the presence of FRET in the N-CD/p-NP system. Thus, the IFE might be the main cause of fluorescence quenching.

The ultraviolet spectra of p-NP and the fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs were studied to further probe the fluorescence reaction mechanism. The absorption peak of the p-NP solution ranged from 350 to 450 nm, which partially overlapped with the excitation and emission spectra of the N-CDs (Fig. 5b). Therefore, the quenching mechanism is caused mainly by the IFE (Zhang, Hassan, et al., 2022). In addition, as the p-NP concentration increased, the fluorescence emission spectrum of the N-CD solution increasingly redshifted (Fig. 4a), which may be due to the interaction between the donor and receptor molecules, affecting their optical properties (Zhang et al., 2019). Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of the N-CDs may be a consequence of the interaction between the IFE and the intermolecular forces between them. Then, the absorption spectra of the N-CD, p-NP, and p-NP-N-CDs mixtures were investigated to further confirm the IFE mechanism. Fig. S4 shows that the absorption curves of the p-NP-N-CDs mixture almost coincide with the sum curves of the N-CD and p-NP mixtures. No p-NP-N-CDs complex was generated, ruling out the possibility of static quenching and thus confirming the IFE quenching mechanism (Li, Xiang, et al., 2023).

Three kinds of real vegetable and fruit samples, namely, apple, cucumber, and lettuce leaves, were analyzed via the N-CD system to further assess the viability of the analytical approach. Standard solutions containing MP at concentrations of 1.00, 3.00, and 5.00 μg·mL−1 were added to real sample solutions, and their fluorescence spectra were determined under optimized conditions. The details of the test results are shown in Table S1. The MP content in vegetable and fruit samples was not determined via an N-CD-based fluorescence sensing system. The recovery of the apples was between 98.7 % and 100.2 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 3.6 %. The recovery of cucumber was between 98.2 % and 100.6 %, and the RSD was less than 3.2 %. The recovery rate of the lettuce leaves was between 98.3 % and 101.9 %, and the RSD was less than 3.4 %. These results indicate the high accuracy of the proposed method. This method can be utilized to analyze real samples.

A facile, green and low-cost approach was used for the preparation of N-CDs via a hydrothermal method utilizing bagasse as a raw material. The QY of the synthesized N-CDs is 32.6 %. An ultrasensitive fluorescence method for the determination of MPs by alkali-induced hydrolysis was established. The quenching action of p-NP on the N-CDs can be explained by the IFE and the interactions among molecules. IFE is the main reason for fluorescence quenching. The practical application of the fluorescence approach in the determination of MP residues in the environment was further studied, and the results were satisfactory. The sensing strategy is fast, simple, low cost, highly sensitive and selective, does not require the use of enzymes, and has good application potential for the determination of MPs in food.

Ji Ma: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Guoxia Huang: Formal analysis. Jie Li: Data curation. Junsheng Li: Supervision. Liujuan Yan: Validation. Jianfeng Wei: Writing – review & editing. Qian Zhang: Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Green Processing of Sugar Resources (GXTZYZR202302) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22265003, 21966008).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.142183.
